title: Planning for Goals
summary: Money feels more meaningful when it's pointed at a goal. The trick is to match each goal to a time horizon, and to work backwards from the target to a monthly amount.

sections.heading: Name the goal, date it, price it
sections.body: A vague 'I should save more' rarely sticks. 'A ₹2 lakh trip in 2 years' or 'a ₹50,000 laptop in 10 months' is concrete — you can work out exactly how much to set aside each month.

sections.heading: Match the goal to a horizon
sections.body: Short-term goals (under ~3 years) belong in safer, stable places so the money is there when you need it. Long-term goals can use growth investments that ride out market ups and downs.

sections.heading: Work backwards
sections.body: Start from the target and the date, then divide into a monthly amount (a SIP-for-a-goal calculation). Automating that monthly transfer makes the goal almost happen by itself.

quiz.prompt: Money for a goal you need in 1 year is best kept in:
The best way to plan a savings goal is to:
options: Save whatever is left at month-end
Work backwards from the target and date to a monthly amount
Wait for a bonus to cover it

quiz.prompt: Which is an example of a well-defined goal as described in the lesson?
A long-term goal (many years away) can be kept in:
options: Growth investments that ride out market ups and downs

quiz.prompt: What makes a monthly goal contribution 'almost happen by itself'?

disclaimer: Educational content only. This is a simulation, not financial advice.
